extracted.brandBackground: Express Employment Professionals is a major staffing franchise offering temporary staffing, contract staffing, and direct hire services.

leadership.name: Robert A. Funk, Jr.
leadership.role: Director, Chairman of the Board, President, and CEO
leadership.background: Appointed CEO in May 2025, previously serving in corporate development and strategic planning roles since 2021.
leadership.whyItMatters: Provides executive leadership and strategic direction for the entire franchise system.

leadership.name: Pat Jones
leadership.role: Chief Financial Officer, Executive Vice President
leadership.background: CFO since September 2022, previously CFO for Petra Industries.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees the financial health and accounting operations of the franchisor.

leadership.name: Russell C. Lissuzzo II
leadership.role: Executive Vice President of Legal and Compliance, General Counsel, and Secretary
leadership.background: General Counsel since January 2022, previously Senior VP of Legal.
leadership.whyItMatters: Manages legal affairs, regulatory compliance, and franchise agreements.

leadership.name: Patty Smith
leadership.background: Appointed EVP of Operations in May 2025, previously Senior VP of Operational Development.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs the operational support and training systems for franchisees.

leadership.name: Vinny Provenzano
leadership.role: Senior Vice President of Global Franchising
leadership.background: SVP of Global Franchising since October 2023, previously VP of Franchising.
leadership.whyItMatters: Leads franchise development and expansion efforts globally.

operationalRisks.description: Disputes must be resolved via mediation, arbitration, or litigation only in Oklahoma.

extracted.conceptRationale: The franchise operates in the professional staffing and recruiting sector, which does not fit standard retail or food categories.
extracted.staffingRationale: Requires a conventional office staff including a Sales Representative, Staffing Consultant, and Front Office Coordinator.

riskReasons: A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.

source.label: Franchise Units Open More Than 24 Months - FY 2025 Sales

underwriting.assessment: $250,000 liquid covers the $209,350 mid-point build-out without financing, though that leaves little working-capital cushion. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $439,612/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: The franchise operates in the professional staffing and recruiting sector, which does not fit standard retail or food categories.

benchmark.label: General franchise (uncategorized)
benchmark.dominantRisk: Cost of goods and labor are not disclosed in any FDD — model them explicitly before trusting any margin figure.
benchmark.rampNote: Assume 6–18 months to a mature run-rate.
considerations: Budget COGS and labor explicitly — neither is in the FDD, and together they usually run 45–65% of sales.
Use the Item 20 franchisee list to validate real operating costs and ramp before committing capital.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 8–20% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: conventionally staffed · ≈ 25.7–45 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “General franchise (uncategorized)” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Taken from the FDD (~$3,125/mo); already netted in margin after fees & rent.
Estimated at the 15–35% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 20–35% conventionally staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor's statements don't show the distress signals we screen for — positive net worth and a clean audit. Still worth a quick confirmation against current figures.
body: What we found (December 28, 2025, audited by RSM US LLP): Net income of $54.38M on $3.23B of revenue — roughly 1.0x spent per $1 earned. Losses are widening, not shrinking. Positive net worth of $619.20M. Cash of $156.70M against $309.64M of obligations due within a year (a current ratio of 2.59).
Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Derived from the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
